references.text: [ErGr80] Erd\H{o}s, P. and Graham, R., Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number theory. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).

remarks.text: This is what I assume the intended problem is, although the presentation in \cite{ErGr80} is missing some crucial quantifiers, so I may have misinterpreted it.

statement.text: Prove the following for all large $x$: there is a choice of congruence classes $a_p$ for all primes $p\leq x$ and a decomposition $\{p\leq x\}=A\sqcup B$ into two non-empty sets such that, for all $n<x$, there exist some $p\in A$ and $q\in B$ such that $n\equiv a_p\pmod{p}$ and $n\equiv a_q\pmod{q}$.
